coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric ﬂuorescence nanoprobe for monitoring of intracellular temperature and tyrosine based on a dual emissive carbon dots/gold nanohybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Jianhao
Mu, Zhao
Hua, Peng
Wang, Meng
Qin, Kunhao
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission nitrogen doped carbon dots/gold nanohybrid (NCDs-Au) was designed for specific and sensitive ratiometric detection of intracellular temperature and tyrosine. In this probe, a reductive NCDs was successfully prepared with the use of natural biomass Dendrobium officinale as precursor. The new prepared NCDs acted as both reducers and stabilizers to synthesize a novel NCDs-Au nanohybrid by a facile one-step procedure along with a quantum yield of 14.3%. The prepared nanoprobe showed characteristic fluorescence emissions of NCDs and Au NCs with single-wavelength excitation. Notably, the nanoprobe shows an interesting wavelength-dependent dual response to temperature (448 nm) and tyrosine (660 nm), enabling the two targets to be detected proportionally. As an effective temperature sensor, the nanoprobe exhibited good temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response from 5 to 75 °C. In addition, the sensor has a linear response toward tyrosine in the range of 0.5–175 μM with a detection limit of 0.19 μM. Moreover, the fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied to ratiometricly monitor the variation of temperature or tyrosine level in cells because of the low cytotoxicity, chemical stability and excellent fluorescence properties. These results suggested that the nanoprobe here has provided the possibility for rapidly biosensing with the acceptable selectivity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Nanothermometer
Tyrosine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and carbon dots/gold nanohybrid
Detection of temperature and tyrosine by NCDs-Au
Fluorescence stability of the NCDs-Au nanohybrid
Ratiometric temperature sensing of NCDs-Au at blue wavelength
Ratiometric detection of Tyr at red wavelength
Live cell imaging of temperature and Tyr

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535).

The surface groups, structure and components of NCDs-Au was studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the FT-IR spectra of Fig. 1d, the functional groups on the surface of NCDs are compared with those of the NCDs-Au nanohybrid. Both NCDs and NCDs-Au exhibited one characteristic peak at about 3440 cm?1, which is ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of O?H and N?H stretching vibrations, As also supported by the peak at 1634 cm?1 originated from the O[dbnd]C-NH stretching vibrations. Similarly, the peak at 1467 cm?1 was attributed to the ?COOH symmetric stretching vibrations. In addition, the peaks at 1119 cm?1 and 1078 cm?1 were responsible for stretching vibration of C?N and C?O. Because of the presence of plentiful hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of the composites, the as-prepared nanohybrid have excellent hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems. In addition, NCDs-Au has a characteristic peak at about 559 cm?1 for the C?Au bonding vibration, but in the NCDs, this peak was inexistent. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1e, the signals of Au4f, C1s, N1s and O1s elements are predominated in XPS survey spectrum of NCDs-Au. High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum displayed four different peaks at the binding energies of 283.1, 284.2, 285.2 and 287.1 eV (Fig. S4a), corresponding to the sp2 C[dbnd]C, C?N, C?O and C[dbnd]O groups, respectively. Similarly, two peaks at 397.8 eV and 399.3 eV of the N1s spectrum (Fig. S4b) were attributed to the C?N?C and N?H bands, respectively. In addition, the two peaks of Au 4f7/2 corresponding to 83.0 and 83.7 eV showed there existed Au(0) and Au(I) for NCDs-Au (Fig. 1f).This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NCDs-Au; (b) HR-TEM image of NCDs-Au; (c) XRD pattern of NCDs-Au; (d) FT-IR spectra of NCDs (black line) and NCDs-Au (red line); (e) XPS spectra of NCDs (blue line) and NCDs-Au (brown line); (f) High-resolution Au4f XPS spectrum of NCDs-Au. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs-Au. Insets: the photographs taken under daylight (A) and UV light of 365 nm (B); (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs and NCDs-Au. Insets: two main absorption peaks in ultraviolet region.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs-Au in the temperature range of 5–75 °C; (b) Photographs of NCDs-Au under UV light (365 nm) at different temperatures during heating and cooling processes; (c) Plot of F448/F660 versus varied temperatures during heating and cooling processes; (d) Reversible fluorescence ratio (F448/F660) changes of NCDs-Au over the range of 5–75 °C.

(A) The fluorescence responses of NCDs-Au towards different interferent (The final concentrations of interferent were held constant at 100 μM); (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs-Au upon addition of Tyr with different concentrations (Inset shows NCDs-Au solution after addition of Tyr under 365 nm UV lamp); (c) Plot of F660/F448 versus concentrations of Tyr from 0.5 to 175 μM.
Viability of HeLa cells and HT-29 cells after incubation of 24 h with different concentrations of the NCDs-Au, as determined by the MTT assay.
CLSM images of (a) HT-29 cells and (b) HeLa cells incubated with NCDs-Au (100 μg/mL) for 2 h, Scale bar: 20 μm.

CLSM images of HT-29 cells incubated with NCDs-Au (100 μg/mL) in blue and red channels, the temperature was varied from 25 to 37 °C, respectively. Scale bar: 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

CLSM images of HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of Tyr in blue and red channels. The cells were incubated with NCDs-Au (100 μg/mL) for 2 h with different concentrations of Tyr (0, 100 μM, 200 μM and 500 μM), Scale bar: 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the preparation of NCDs-Au.
The proposed fluorescence sensing mechanism for ratiometric assay of Tyr.
Analytical results for the Determination of Tyr in human serum.
Precision (% RSD) and Accuracy (%) of the method in human serum.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Low, Mid and High represent, 20%, 50% and 80% levels of the working range of the method respectively.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric ﬂuorescence nanoprobe for monitoring of intracellular temperature and tyrosine based on a dual emissive carbon dots/gold nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Gedian Humanwell Pharmaceutical Co.,Ltd, Wuhan, Hubei Province, 430206, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Land Resource Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province, 650500, China

sa:organization.ce:italic: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission nitrogen doped carbon dots/gold nanohybrid (NCDs-Au) was designed for specific and sensitive ratiometric detection of intracellular temperature and tyrosine. In this probe, a reductive NCDs was successfully prepared with the use of natural biomass Dendrobium officinale as precursor. The new prepared NCDs acted as both reducers and stabilizers to synthesize a novel NCDs-Au nanohybrid by a facile one-step procedure along with a quantum yield of 14.3%. The prepared nanoprobe showed characteristic fluorescence emissions of NCDs and Au NCs with single-wavelength excitation. Notably, the nanoprobe shows an interesting wavelength-dependent dual response to temperature (448 nm) and tyrosine (660 nm), enabling the two targets to be detected proportionally. As an effective temperature sensor, the nanoprobe exhibited good temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response from 5 to 75 °C. In addition, the sensor has a linear response toward tyrosine in the range of 0.5–175 μM with a detection limit of 0.19 μM. Moreover, the fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied to ratiometricly monitor the variation of temperature or tyrosine level in cells because of the low cytotoxicity, chemical stability and excellent fluorescence properties. These results suggested that the nanoprobe here has provided the possibility for rapidly biosensing with the acceptable selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are known to be precursors of various important biological substances. Tyrosine (4-hydroxyphenylalanine, Tyr) is an essential aromatic amino acid, an important part of protein, and is indispensable for establishing and maintaining a positive nitrogen balance in human nutrition [1,2]. Tyr can be used as a precursor of many neurotransmitters, including l-dopa, dopamine, norepinephrine and epinephrine. It can also be used as a precursor of melanin, thyroid hormone and several opioid peptides. The terminal amino group has a tyrosyl residue [3,4]. Normal human plasma tyrosine levels are 35–90 μM. Abnormal changes in the amount of Tyr relative to normal levels are directly related to certain diseases. Its abnormal increase can cause hereditary tyrosinemia, Alzheimer's disease and cancer, while a lack of Tyr can lead to albinism and albuminuria [5,6]. Therefore, the qualitative and quantitative analysis of Tyr and the research on cell imaging are of practical significance for the prevention and diagnosis of diseases, and monitoring the Tyr level in the body at any time. So far, various kinds of analytical techniques have been applied to detect the Tyr content, such as liquid chromatographic-tandem mass spectrometric (LC-MS) [7], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], electrochemical techniques [4]. However, some shortcomings of these methods may arise due to the time-consuming, tedious sample pretreatment, large material consumption and expensive equipment. Moreover, there have been few reports on fluorescent cell imaging of Tyr. Therefore, it seems particularly necessary to develop simple, fast, highly sensitive and selective probes for detecting Tyr and further application in cell imaging research. Likewise, as a basic physiological characteristic, intracellular temperature regulates biochemical interactions in living cells. Intracellular heat generation is responsible for the biological reaction rate and the diffusion process of biomolecules [10]. Accordingly, intracellular thermal sensing would be of great significance in revealing a variety of cellular activities, elucidating various biological phenomena and promoting the development of cell biology [11]. Recently, the measurement of intracellular temperature is extensively performed by using bioimaging. Generally, bioimaging with polymeric nano-thermometers or organic dyes has limitations in biological medium because of their toxic nature and laborious synthesis steps [12–14]. Hence, monitoring the intracellular tyrosine and temperature with a novel fluorescence (FL) nanoprobe is still a hot topic.

As a new class of optical materials, fluorescent noble metal nanoclusters show absorption and emission in the blue to near-infrared region(NIR). Furthermore, they have became promising optical label in chemical/biosensing applications because of their unique advantages, reasonable surface chemistry design and excellent surface/interface recognition capabilities [15]. Among the precious metals, gold nanoclusters(Au NCs) stand out due to their ease of synthesis, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [16]. To alter the applications of Au NCs, the reducing agent and stabilizer should be utilized for improving various factors.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of carbon materials in recent years, open a new field for fluorescence sensing and imaging. Particularly, they have excellent properties, including excellent fluorescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, strong chemical inertness, and ease of preparation [17,18]. What's more, CDs also can be used as reductant and stabilizer, which can reduce the Au+, Ag+ or Cu2+ to metallic nanoparticles [19,20]. Since they are excellent electron acceptors or electron donors, and their large surface area, many attempts have recently been made to prepare CDs-Au nanocomposites [21,22]. However, a few dual emissive nanosensor based on CDs-Au nanocomposites for assaying multiple analytes have been reported before [23,24]. Consequently, the above-mentioned works encourages us to design and synthesize dual emissive CDs-Au nanohybrid as sensor for assaying multiple targets.

In this report, we developed a dual-emission nanoprobe based on nitrogen doped carbon dots/gold nanohybrid (NCDs-Au) for ratiometric detection of intracellular temperature and tyrosine. Firstly, reductive nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) was successfully prepared using biomass Dendrobium officinale (D. officinale) as precursor by single-step hydrothermal method. D. officinale has been widely used in medicinal herb or functional foods for thousands of years in China. The main bioactive substances of the plant are coumarin, alkaloids, polysaccharides [25]. The dual emissive NCDs-Au nanohybrid was synthesized by a one-step procedure and do not need further processing and any additional reducers and stabilizers. This probe possesses two emission peaks at 448 nm (blue) and 660 nm (red) under a single excitation wavelength of 362 nm. The FL intensity of NCDs-Au at blue wavelength decreased gradually and regularly with increasing the temperature, and showed temperature-dependent fluorescence properties. The FL intensity at red wavelength increased remarkably after the addition of Tyr into the assay system. Further, in the cellular study, this nanoprobe showed excellent cells viability and applicability in the detection of temperature and tyrosine in HT-29 cells and HeLa cells, demonstrating their potential in biosensor fields. To our best knowledge, this is the first report regarding the ratiometric detection of intracellular temperature and tyrosine.
Details including chemicals, instruments and the fluorescence quantum yield determination can be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and carbon dots/gold nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of NCDs prepared through hydrothermal method according to a reported method [26]. As shown in Scheme 1 , typically, the stems of D. officinale was dried naturally at room temperature and then ground into a fine powder. 2.0 g prepared powder, 90 mL hot water (60 °C) were added into a flask with constant stirring to uniformly dispersed. Ethanol (10 mL) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 1 mL) were added into above flask before transferring the mixture into Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 9 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The yellow aqueous solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove undissolved substances and large particles. Finally, the obtained NCDs was dispersed as a stock solution (2.0 mg/mL) in purified water for further characterization and use.

According to the reported references [27], with minor modification (shown in Scheme 1), the preparation of carbon dots/gold nanohybrid was as follows:10 mL of prepared NCDs stock solution was taken into round-bottomed flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer and a reflux condenser and heated to 100 °C under steady stirring. 100 μL of 0.1 mg/mL HAuCl4·4H2O solution were added to flask, the reaction flask was kept at 100 °C until the color of the solution was observed to change from light yellow to brown (about 10 min), indicating the formation of NCDs-Au.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of temperature and tyrosine by NCDs-Au
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, thermal sensing study was performed by heating and cooling the solution of NCDs-Au (2.0 μg/mL) in water bath at different temperatures. The solution was set at various temperature ranges from 5 to 75 °C. The corresponding FL intensity of the NCDs-Au was measured by excitation at 362 nm, slit width 5 nm (both emission and excitation). The enhancement effect of Tyr on FL of NCDs-Au was conducted as follows: The NCDs-Au dispersion solution was diluted with the citric acid and dibasic sodium phosphate buffer solution (CA-Na2HPO4 system, 20 mM, pH 6.0) to a final concentration of 2.0 μg/mL. Next, 20 μL of Tyr at various concentrations were added into 3.0 mL such solution separately, followed by vigorous shaking on a vortex mixer and incubation at 25 °C for 2 min. The FL spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 362 nm and excitation/emission slits of 5 nm. In a similar way, other kinds of amino acid molecules, metal ions, glucose and glutathione solutions were selected to explore the selectivity of the ratiometric nanoprobe toward Tyr.

For investigating the practicability, the detection was carried out in human serum samples. Human serum samples were obtained from Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province. The authors point out that all experiments were performed in accordance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines. For Tyr detection in human serum, Tyr with different concentration was spiked in 10-fold diluted human serum samples before analysis, and all detection procedure was the same as the experiment for Tyr detection in buffer.

The 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed to study the biocompatibility of NCDs-Au. HeLa cells and HT-29 cells were used as model cells and incubated in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM). Typically, HeLa cells and HT-29 cells harvested in the logarithmic growth phase were first transferred into a 96-well plate to provide a cell density of 7000 cells per well. After incubating at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h, the medium was replaced with fresh medium, and then different concentrations of NCDs-Au were added. The cells were cultured for another 24 h under the same conditions. Then, the medium was replaced with fresh medium again, and MTT in PBS solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After reacting at 37 °C for 4 h, the supernatant was removed, and 150 μL DMSO was added to dissolve the obtained MTT formazan crystals. Finally, the plate was shaken for 10 min and the absorbance of the formazan product was measured at 570 nm by a multi-well plate reader. The optical density of the control group not receiving NCDs-Au represented 100% growth of the HeLa cells and HT-29 cells.

To measure the intracellular temperature in live cells, HT-29 cells was conducted by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). Cells was grown on a 35 mm glass culture dish under humidified incubation conditions at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After 24 h, cells were treated with NCDs-Au (100 μg/mL) and maintained under similar incubation conditions. Prior to imaging under CLSM, cells were gently washed with PBS. In CLSM, the temperature varies from 25 to 37 °C. Few representative cells were selected to evaluate changes in FL intensity with temperature.

HeLa cells was chosen as model organisms for evaluating the feasibility of NCDs-Au in cell imaging of Tyrosine. To verify the identification of Tyr in cells, HeLa cells was grown on a 35 mm glass culture dish and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The nanoprobe with a concentration of 100 μg/mL were added to the cell culture for incubation 6 h at 37 °C, and then washed three times by PBS buffer to eliminate the remaining probes. Subsequently, exogenous Tyr with different concentrations were added directly to the Petri dish and incubated for 2 h. After washed three times with fresh PBS buffer, HeLa cells were observed using CLSM. All cells were imaged under an excitation of 405 nm, and the emissions were acquired separately with two different single band fluorescence filters.

The NCDs-Au was synthesized in two steps as shown in Scheme 1. The design of the NCDs-Au nanohybrid was based on the fact that the NCDs acts as the reducing and stabilizing agents. In our work, a fluorescent CDs was prepared using the natural biomass medicinal material D. officinale through hydrothermal carbonization technique. Unfortunately, the obtained CDs shows a lower fluorescence quantum yield (QY), which limits its practical applicability. In a typical synthesis, it has been proved that doping CDs with nitrogen atoms is a feasible method to improve the QY of CDs, because the nitrogen doping process can adjust the inherent electronic and surface characteristics of CDs, giving CDs new properties, such as the ability to react with metal ions or biomolecules have high-sensitivity sensing capabilities [28,29]. Thus, as a nitrogen-containing precursor, EDA was employed to produce NCDs with high QY values. The QY of the prepared NCDs was approximately 25.6%. In the process of reduction of HAuCl4·4H2O, the synthesized NCDs do not require further processing or surface modification and have an excellent reducibility. Au atoms can be covalently bonded to the surface of NCDs steadily. C and N atoms are the bonding sites with Au atoms, except Au–C bond forming, the Au–N bond formed via the –NH2 group also plays the significant bonding role in the process of formation of NCDs-Au nanohybrids. Addition of HAuCl4·4H2O the NCDs and further heating of the solution results in the reduction of AuCl4 − ions, which was evident from the change in color of the solution to brown. After the introduction of gold atoms, the fluorescence of the NCDs was slightly quenched, while a new emission peak is obtained in the red region (660 nm) (Fig. S1). The quantum yield of the NCDs-Au (QY = 14.3%) was much lower than that of the NCDs.

The morphology of the NCDs-Au was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2, the results show that the NCDs-Au are well-dispersed spherical dots with average diameters of 5–6 nm (Fig. S3). The HR-TEM image of NCDs-Au (Fig. 1b) shows the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm agreed with that of the (100) facet of graphite, and 0.24 nm spacing was ascribed to the Au (111) [21], indicating that the NCDs-Au have been successfully synthesized. Again, the XRD pattern of NCDs-Au shows the diffraction peak located at 38.2°, 44.2°, 64.1°and 77.5° (Fig. 1c), indicating the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of metallic Au. Furthermore, the broad diffraction peak at 21.8° is the characteristic peak of carbon, which further demonstrates the formation of NCDs-Au nanohybrid.

The surface groups, structure and components of NCDs-Au was studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the FT-IR spectra of Fig. 1d, the functional groups on the surface of NCDs are compared with those of the NCDs-Au nanohybrid. Both NCDs and NCDs-Au exhibited one characteristic peak at about 3440 cm−1, which is ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, As also supported by the peak at 1634 cm−1 originated from the OC-NH stretching vibrations. Similarly, the peak at 1467 cm−1 was attributed to the –COOH symmetric stretching vibrations. In addition, the peaks at 1119 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 were responsible for stretching vibration of C–N and C–O. Because of the presence of plentiful hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of the composites, the as-prepared nanohybrid have excellent hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems. In addition, NCDs-Au has a characteristic peak at about 559 cm−1 for the C–Au bonding vibration, but in the NCDs, this peak was inexistent. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1e, the signals of Au4f, C1s, N1s and O1s elements are predominated in XPS survey spectrum of NCDs-Au. High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum displayed four different peaks at the binding energies of 283.1, 284.2, 285.2 and 287.1 eV (Fig. S4a), corresponding to the sp2 CC, C–N, C–O and CO groups, respectively. Similarly, two peaks at 397.8 eV and 399.3 eV of the N1s spectrum (Fig. S4b) were attributed to the C–N–C and N–H bands, respectively. In addition, the two peaks of Au 4f7/2 corresponding to 83.0 and 83.7 eV showed there existed Au(0) and Au(I) for NCDs-Au (Fig. 1f).

To get an insight into the optical properties of the NCDs-Au, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra have been successively investigated. The NCDs-Au exhibited distinctly two emission peaks at around 448 nm and 660 nm upon 362 nm excitation (Fig. 2 a). The aqueous solution of NCDs-Au emitted obvious blue fluorescence (Fig. 2a, photograph B) under UV light (365 nm), while appeared as light brown under daylight (Fig. 2a, photograph A). Meanwhile, the emission peak in the blue region of NCDs-Au exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. S5, when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak shifts significantly to a longer wavelength as the FL intensity changes. The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs (Fig. 2b) possess a peaks at 278 nm and a shoulder at 330 nm, the peak at 278 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 CC bonds, while a shoulder at 330 nm should be attributed to n-π * transition of CO bonds on the surface of the NCDs [30]. The NCDs-Au showed a more intensive absorption in the range below 400 nm due to the introduction of gold atoms, but no characteristic surface plasmon resonance absorption peak appearing at the range of visible light, indicating no big gold nanoparticles were formed. In other words, the NCDs-Au nanohybrid were formed. Accordingly, the above characterizations suggested that dual emissive fluorescence NCDs-Au nanohybrid was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of the NCDs-Au nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the stability of NCDs-Au for bearing circumstances, corresponding experiments were performed. As revealed in Figs. S6a and b, the FL intensity of NCDs-Au hardly exhibited variation along with varying concentrations of NaCl and continuous excitation. In addition, the FL intensity of NCDs-Au was investigated under various pH solutions (2.0–8.0). As demonstrated in Fig. S6c, the FL intensity is relatively stable in the interval 2.0–6.0. However, the FL intensity decreases dramatically in basic media. All of these effects enable the NCDs-Au to be used as a favorable sensing probe in the acidic medium especially in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric temperature sensing of NCDs-Au at blue wavelength
ce:section.ce:para: As a pivotal physical parameters, temperature plays an important role in chemical and biological systems, such as cellular behaviors and chemical equilibrium [31]. In our work, an interesting dependence between the FL intensity and temperature of NCDs-Au was firstly investigated. Fig. 3 a and b shows an inverse relationship between FL intensity of NCDs-Au at blue wavelength with the temperature change. As shown in Fig. 3a, the FL intensity of NCDs-Au at around 448 nm decrease gradually and regularly with increasing the temperature from 5 to 75 °C, while that of NCDs-Au at 660 nm are nearly unchanged. Under the condition of increasing temperature, the blue wavelength of NCDs-Au fluorescence turn off. Its advantage is different from turn on. It is a form of fluorescence quenching. In order to further understand the relationship between temperature and the FL intensity of NCDs-Au, a good linear relationship was obtained between FL intensity ratio (F448/F660) and temperature (5–75 °C) by plotting the FL intensity ratio (F448/F660) versus temperature. As shown in Fig. 3c, the red line represents the change in F448/F660 during the heating process (R2 = 0.997), while the blue one represents cooling processes (R2 = 0.991). The efficiency and stability of temperature sensing system was further tested by changing the temperature of NCDs-Au solution from low temperature (5 °C) to 75 °C, and then recording the FL intensity ratio (F448/F660) (Fig. 3d), the ratio value still maintains constant even after five heating-cooling cycles. All of excellent property suggested NCDs-Au can be served as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for reversibly sensing temperature. According to the previous literatures, the temperature-dependent fluorescence characteristics originate from temperature-enhanced populations of non-radiative channels in the surface (trap/defect) state. Based on the exciton-phonon coupling mechanism, the FL intensity of CDs decreases significantly with increasing temperature, which is attributed to thermally activated non-radiative capture. At low temperatures, the non-radiative channel is not thermally activated. Therefore, excited electrons can radiate photons. However, once the temperature increases, more non-radiative channels will be thermally activated, and more and more excited electrons will return to the ground state through non-radiative processes, which will cause the quantum yield and FL intensity to decrease [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of Tyr at red wavelength
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, a novel ratiometric fluorescence assay for the detection of Tyr was developed. In order to get the best Tyr sensing performance, the experimental conditions were studied. Taking into account the complexity of a real intracellular system, we have investigated the influence of various potential interfering substances on the FL intensity, including amino acids molecules (leucine (Leu), glutamic acid (Glu), tryptophan (Trp), aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), glutamine (Gln), arginine (Arg)), ions (Cl−, I−, Na+, K+, Fe2+ and Fe3+), Glutathione (GSH) and glucose (Glc). As shown in Fig. 4 a, most of these substances have nearly no interference to the FL intensity ratio (F660/F448) even at a high concentration (100 μM), the results revealed that the ratiometric probe shows selectivity and specificity for Tyr over the other interferent.

The assay conditions for the detection of Tyr was optimized to obtain a high sensitivity. Based on the analysis of the effects of various pH on NCDs-Au (Fig. S6c), since the pH of the reaction solution has a significant influence on the NCDs-Au nanoprobe, the variations in fluorescence response towards Tyr were investigated over the pH range of 2.0–7.4. The detection linear range, coefficient of determination (R2) and the limit of detection were listed in Table S1. Considering that the environment in the cells is weakly acidic, pH 6.0 was chosen as the optimal pH for Tyr detection. The effect of response time on the FL intensity of the ratiometric probe was also considered. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence at 660 nm increased remarkably and then stabilized within 2 min, while the emission at 448 nm was only slightly affected, therefore, the response time of 2 min was utilized in subsequent experiments.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4b, we found that after the addition of Tyr into the obtained NCDs-Au system, the fluorescence at 660 nm increased remarkably while the emission at 448 nm is only slightly affected, making the increase of the F660/F448 value. Fluorescence turn on occurs at the red wavelength of NCDs-Au, which is a form of fluorescence enhancement. Meanwhile, the color of NCDs-Au system showed blue from heavy to light under UV light as the concentration of Tyr increased by degrees, then, pink fluorescence emerged at the end of this process. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, there has a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.995) between the F660/F448 value and the concentration of Tyr in the range of 0.5–175 μM, the limit of detection was approximately 0.19 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). In addition, in order to study the reliability of the present assay under real conditions, we used the developed nanoprobe to detect Tyr in human serum samples, recovery studies were carried out by spiking the samples with various amounts of Tyr. The acceptable results were listed in Table 1 , satisfactory recoveries in the range of 96.16–103.03% were obtained with the RSDs of 2.45–4.33%. As shown in Table 2 , the precision of the method is evaluated by the same analyst under repeatable conditions (intra-batch accuracy) and repeatable conditions on the same day; by two analysts in a two-week stepwise order in random order on different dates (inter-batch accuracy). For two samples, the precision of the method is expressed as a percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD) of the concentration, which is less than 2.4%, and the obtained recovery value in human serum is 96.6–102.1%. Therefore, the analytical performance of the near-infrared ratiometric fluorescence probe offers a promising alternative for precise measurements of Tyr in real samples [34,35]. We compared the performance of the developed method with the previously reported methods of temperature and tyrosine sensing. Table S2 shows that there are many reports about temperature and Tyr sensing methods. However, the detection of temperature and tyrosine using ratiometric fluorescence method is the first report. At the same time, the linear range of this method is relatively wide and has a lower limit of detection. Although we can't ratiometricly monitor the variation of temperature and Tyr simultaneously in solution or intracellular systems, it still shows that this research method has certain significance.

Based on the phenomenon that the FL intensity at red region increased remarkably after the addition of Tyr into the obtained NCDs-Au system, the fluorescence enhancement may be due to strong interaction between Tyr and small gold nanoparticles [36]. To clarify the mechanism for NCDs-Au sensing Tyr, further explorations such as FT-IR, FL, TEM, UV–vis absorption spectra before and after the addition of Tyr have been designed. As shown in Fig. S8, FT-IR spectra of NCDs-Au before and after the addition of Tyr were recorded, their main infrared absorption peaks showed no obvious difference, suggesting no interaction between Tyr and NCDs. According to the comparison between Fig. 1a and Fig. S9, the FL intensity of NCDs-Au at red region can be greatly enhanced by Tyr (Fig. 4b), there was no significant change in particle size and stability, indicating that the change in FL did not originate from a change in particle state. UV–vis absorption spectra were further recorded (Fig. S10). After adding Tyr, the absorbance in the range of 200–300 nm increased. The large peak at 278 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds of Tyr can prove the strong interaction between Tyr and small gold nanoparticles. Furthermore, according to the literatures, Tyr has been employed as unique reducing agents for the synthesis of gold and silver nanoparticles [37,38]. Tyr was the unique who possess the reducing phenolic –OH group out of more than 20 kinds of amino acid. There existed Au (0) and Au (I) for NCDs-Au on the basis of XPS analysis (Fig. 1f), the phenolic –OH group is prime accountable reducing group for the reduction of Au (I) to Au (0) and the functional groups (-COOH and –NH2) of Tyr tend to establish strong links with small gold nanoparticles [39–41]. The synergy effect can promote the generation of larger chromophores and UV absorbance, which produce more intense emission under same excitation (shown in Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live cell imaging of temperature and Tyr
ce:section.ce:para: We utilized HT-29 cells as a model to study the temperature-dependent fluorescence imaging of NCDs-Au using CLSM. The nanoprobe with a concentration of 100 μg/mL were added to the cell culture for incubation 2 h before imaging under CLSM. As shown in Fig. 7 , NCDs-Au with clear fluorescence emission disperse well within living cells in blue and red channels, indicating the prepared NCDs-Au can be used to label HT-29 cells. The nanoprobe internalized in HT-29 cells showed intense fluorescence at 25 °C which gradually decreased as temperature is changed from 25 °C to 30 °C–37 °C in blue channel. The fluorescence of nanoprobe in blue channel was recovered over temperature decreased to 25 °C, while the FL intensity was almost the same in red channel, indicating that it can be used to detect the temperature variation in the cellular environment. Furthermore, the feasibility of NCDs-Au for live cell imaging of Tyr was then explored. Fig. 8 shows intracellular Tyr sensing of HeLa cells preincubated with NCDs-Au, bright fluorescence of the nanoprobe in HeLa cells is observed in blue and red channels. When the concentration of Tyr changed from 0 to 500 μM, the FL intensity of the red channel gradually increased, and the FL intensity of the blue channel remained almost constant in the presence of different concentrations of Tyr, indicating that the prepared NCDs-Au can be used to monitor Tyr concentration changes in HeLa cells.

In summary, we have described a green, facile, and economic route for fabricating a reductive NCDs with the use of natural biomass Dendrobium officinale as precursor. The new prepared NCDs acted as both reducers and stabilizers to synthesize a novel dual-emissive NCDs-Au nanohybrid by a facile one-step procedure. The prepared nanoprobe possesses two emission peaks at 448 nm (blue) and 660 nm (red) under a single excitation wavelength of 362 nm. Remarkably, the dual emissive fluorescence nanoprobe can be employed for differentiation of temperature and tyrosine in a ratiometric method from two separated channels. The nanoprobe as an effective temperature sensor exhibited good temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response from 5 to 75 °C in blue channel, and it showed a high selectivity detection of tyrosine with a wide linear 0.5–175 μM in red channel. More importantly, the fluorescent nanoprobe can be applied to ratiometricly monitor the variation of temperature or tyrosine level in cells. Therefore, the present ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be regarded as a potential detection method for intracellular temperature and tyrosine sensing.

Jianhao Hua: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zhao Mu: Methodology, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Peng Hua: Resources, Validation. Meng Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Kunhao Qin: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121279.
